summary: Joel Spolsky discusses Stack Exchange's approach to growing its network of 51 online communities, arguing that algorithmic approaches alone fail at community building. He observes that each community has unique needs requiring human judgment rather than one-size-fits-all software solutions. To address the challenge of scaling community cultivation, he announces plans to build a team of 'super-evangelists' who will learn community-building skills through intensive daily training. The post doubles as a job listing for this new community evangelism team.
key_points: Algorithmic approaches to community building fail because each community has distinct needs and values that require human judgment
Many technology companies mistake building software for building communities, then wonder why people don't show up
Critical mass—the point where a community grows organically—requires active cultivation, not just a platform
Stack Exchange plans to train community evangelists through an intensive daily program combining hands-on work with study of marketing and community-building literature
The skill of turning a dead internet site into a thriving community is a distinctly 21st-century profession that doesn't yet have an established training path
money_quotes: Pouring a big fat algorithm in equal measures on top of 51 different groups of people does not always work the way you might hope it would work.
Maybe that's why the super-algorithm companies (like Google) tend to suck when they try to build social applications.

There are an awful lot of technology companies, founded by programmers, who think they are building communities on the Internet, but they're really just building software and wondering why the community doesn't magically show up.
You can't use a robot to train a puppy. Every puppy is different.
You need smart human beings to use smart human judgment and cultivate each community individually.
Critical mass is that magic moment when the community has enough activity that it grows by itself.
Everyone who joins the program (and survives for a year) will come out with an almost supernatural ability to take a dead, lifeless site on the internet and make it into the hottest bar in town.
key_insight: Building online communities is fundamentally a human endeavor requiring individual judgment and cultivation, not an engineering problem solvable with algorithms.
